expectations.evidence: Transcript shows a formatted table in Phase 1 Step 2 with columns #, Category, Recommendation, Rationale, Status covering Frontend, Backend, Database, Auth, Payments, AI/LLM, Deployment, and Extras. A confirmation table is also shown in Step 4.

expectations.text: Recognizes this is an internal tool and factors that into framework choice (SPA is fine, SSR not strictly needed)
expectations.evidence: Transcript explicitly states 'Internal dashboard with auth, database, and Stripe webhooks' and chooses NextAuth because it is 'Self-hosted, free for internal tools' and Railway because it is 'good for internal tools with databases'. The Backend row notes 'Next.js API routes sufficient' (no separate backend), reflecting internal-tool simplicity. While it chose Next.js (which has SSR capability), the rationale is 'App Router + API routes, ideal for dashboards' -- framing it as a dashboard tool, not an SEO-driven SSR choice.

expectations.text: Generates auth boilerplate (middleware, provider config, or similar)
expectations.evidence: Files exist: src/lib/auth.ts (NextAuth config with credentials provider, JWT callbacks), src/middleware.ts (auth middleware protecting /dashboard routes), src/app/api/auth/[...nextauth]/route.ts (NextAuth route handler). Auth is fully configured with credential verification against the DB.

expectations.text: Generates a Stripe webhook handler file
expectations.evidence: File exists: src/app/api/webhooks/stripe/route.ts. Contains POST handler with signature verification, handles payment_intent.succeeded, payment_intent.payment_failed, and charge.refunded events with DB updates.

expectations.text: Generates a database schema with order/fulfillment-relevant tables
expectations.evidence: src/lib/db/schema.ts defines orders table (with fields: id, externalOrderId, customerEmail, customerName, status with enum [pending/processing/shipped/delivered/cancelled], totalAmountCents, currency) and payments table (with stripePaymentIntentId, stripeChargeId, status with enum [pending/succeeded/failed/refunded], amountCents). Also has users and sessions tables for auth.

expectations.text: Generates .env.example with Stripe-related env vars (STRIPE_SECRET_KEY, STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET, etc.)
expectations.evidence: .env.example file exists and contains STRIPE_SECRET_KEY=sk_test_... and STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET=whsec_... along with DATABASE_URL, AUTH_SECRET, and AUTH_URL.

expectations.text: Files are wired together — webhook handler uses the db instance, auth protects routes, etc.
expectations.evidence: Webhook handler (src/app/api/webhooks/stripe/route.ts) imports db from '@/lib/db' and schema from '@/lib/db/schema', uses Drizzle queries to update payments/orders. Auth config (src/lib/auth.ts) imports db and users schema to verify credentials. Middleware (src/middleware.ts) imports auth from '@/lib/auth' and protects /dashboard routes. The stripe client (src/lib/stripe.ts) is imported by the webhook handler. All integrations are connected.
